title: Fitting a wall batten shelf to masonry or stud
subtitle: Fix a timber shelf board to a wall using a rear batten on masonry or stud fixings
excerpt: How to fit a timber shelf to a wall using a rear support batten, covering locating studs or drilling masonry, fixing the batten level, cutting the shelf to length, and finishing the front edge.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Carpentry for Boys (1914). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal timber strip fixed to the wall that supports the underside of a shelf along its full rear edge. The batten carries the vertical load directly into the wall fixings. A 50 x 25 mm PAR batten is the standard for shelves up to 250 mm deep carrying books or kitchenware.

A plastic or nylon sleeve inserted into a drilled masonry hole to grip a screw thread. The plug expands as the screw is driven, clamping against the sides of the hole. Red plugs take No. 8 screws, brown plugs take No. 10 screws. Use a SDS drill for masonry holes above 6 mm diameter.

content.text: carries all the load from a shelf directly into the wall fixings. A 50 x 25 mm PAR batten, fixed with three screws into masonry or into studs, supports a 250 mm deep shelf carrying a full load of books. The method works on both masonry and stud walls; only the fixing method changes. The key is a level batten: any tilt in the batten appears doubled at the front edge of a long shelf.

The shelf board: 18 mm or 25 mm PAR softwood or hardwood, cut to the required length. The batten: 50 x 25 mm PAR, cut to fit between the shelf end marks on the wall (or the full shelf width if the ends are open). For masonry: No. 10 x 70 mm screws and red wall plugs, one fixing every 300 mm. For stud walls: No. 8 x 63 mm screws driven into the studs at 600 mm centres.
Step 1, mark the shelf position and locate fixings

Mark the shelf height on the wall with the pencil. On a stud wall, tap across the wall to find the hollow sound of the spaces between studs and the solid thump over a stud: studs run at 400 or 600 mm centres. Confirm with a stud finder or a small pilot-drill hole behind where the batten will cover. On masonry, drill into the brick or block rather than the mortar joint: mortar is weaker than the masonry units and
in mortar can pull out under load.

Hold the batten against the wall at the shelf height mark. Place the spirit level on top of the batten and check it is level. Mark through the batten fixing holes onto the wall. For masonry: drill the marked holes with the masonry bit (use the SDS drill for holes above 6 mm), push in the wall plugs flush with the wall surface, and drive the screws through the batten into the plugs. For stud walls: drive screws directly into the studs. Pull the batten tight and check level again before tightening fully.
Step 3, cut and fit the shelf board

Cut the shelf to the required length with the crosscut saw. The shelf sits on the batten and butts against the wall at the back. Fix the shelf to the batten with two No. 8 x 38 mm screws driven up through the batten into the underside of the shelf, one near each end. Pre-drill the batten to avoid splitting. Countersink the shelf face at each screw point if driving screws from above.

The front edge of the shelf takes the most wear. Round it with the sanding block through 80 grit and 120 grit: a 2 to 3 mm radius removes the sharp corner without looking heavy. Fill any screw holes in the top face, sand smooth, and apply the chosen finish: a hard wax oil for a natural look or two coats of paint for a painted room.

Side battens on the wall, one at each end of the shelf, allow the shelf to be supported from three sides. This is useful in an alcove, where the side walls provide extra support. A front batten fixed to the underside of a deep shelf stops it sagging at mid-span. For a floating-shelf look, use a proprietary hidden bracket instead of an exposed batten; the bracket is a steel rod that slides into a routed slot in the back edge of the shelf.

Check the batten screws annually if the shelf carries heavy loads. Screws in masonry plugs can loosen if the wall surface is powdery or if the plug was undersized. Tap the batten with a fist: any movement means a fixing has loosened. Replace a loose plug with the next size up (brown No. 10 instead of red No. 8) and a longer screw.

attrs.symptom: Shelf slopes visibly toward the front after fixing
attrs.cause: The batten was fixed with a slight tilt, leaning forward from the wall; or the wall behind is not plumb.
attrs.fix: Loosen the batten screws and pack behind the top edge with thin card or timber shims until the spirit level reads level. Re-drive the screws and check again.

attrs.symptom: Wall plug pulls out when the shelf is loaded
attrs.cause: The plug was drilled into soft mortar rather than brick or block, or the hole was drilled oversize for the plug.
attrs.fix: Re-drill the hole in a different position into the masonry unit. Use a brown No. 10 plug and a 70 mm screw for a heavier shelf. If the wall is a cavity wall with a dry-lined face, use a toggle or plasterboard anchor rated for the load.
